coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots encapsulated in (YGd)2O3:Eu3+ nanophosphors for thermal sensing, advanced forensic and information security applications

coredata.dc:description: The outstanding optical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) make them an extremely promising class of multifunctional nanomaterials based on carbon. The incorporation of hydrothermally prepared Aloe vera carbon dots (AVCDs) into europium doped yttrium gadolinium ((YGd)2O3:Eu3+) mixed oxide nanophosphors ((YGO):Eu3+ NPs) synthesized through the solution combustion (SC) method. The resulting (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs nanocomposite (NCs) demonstrates a broad excitation band, with the intense peak at ∼395 nm, aligning with the emission wavelength (350–420 nm) of n-UV LED chips. Notably, the (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs exhibits a 4 fold increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity compared to (YGO):7Eu3+, attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between AVCDs and Eu3+ ions. Remarkably, even at an elevated temperature of 423 K, the thermal stability of (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) remains exceptional, retaining 88.31 % of its initial luminescence intensity. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is measured at a notable 74.16 %, while the optimized composite achieves a remarkable color purity of 99.59 %. Latent fingerprints (LFPs) are effectively developed on different surfaces using the optimized NCs via a simple powder dusting technique, and these fingerprints (FPs) revealed under 365 nm UV light. The study also examines various pore parameter namely size, shape, number, position, type, and inter-distance. Moreover, a transparent anti-counterfeiting (AC) ink is fabricated using optimized NCs providing effective AC features through its red emission under 365 nm UV light. This study not only offers solutions for overcoming the self-quenching effect of CDs but also underscores the potential applications in white light emitting diodes (w-LEDs), latent fingerprints, lip prints (LPs), and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The outstanding optical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) make them an extremely promising class of multifunctional nanomaterials based on carbon. The incorporation of hydrothermally prepared Aloe vera carbon dots (AVCDs) into europium doped yttrium gadolinium ((YGd)2O3:Eu3+) mixed oxide nanophosphors ((YGO):Eu3+ NPs) synthesized through the solution combustion (SC) method. The resulting (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs nanocomposite (NCs) demonstrates a broad excitation band, with the intense peak at ∼395 nm, aligning with the emission wavelength (350–420 nm) of n-UV LED chips. Notably, the (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs exhibits a 4 fold increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity compared to (YGO):7Eu3+, attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between AVCDs and Eu3+ ions. Remarkably, even at an elevated temperature of 423 K, the thermal stability of (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) remains exceptional, retaining 88.31 % of its initial luminescence intensity. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is measured at a notable 74.16 %, while the optimized composite achieves a remarkable color purity of 99.59 %. Latent fingerprints (LFPs) are effectively developed on different surfaces using the optimized NCs via a simple powder dusting technique, and these fingerprints (FPs) revealed under 365 nm UV light. The study also examines various pore parameter namely size, shape, number, position, type, and inter-distance. Moreover, a transparent anti-counterfeiting (AC) ink is fabricated using optimized NCs providing effective AC features through its red emission under 365 nm UV light. This study not only offers solutions for overcoming the self-quenching effect of CDs but also underscores the potential applications in white light emitting diodes (w-LEDs), latent fingerprints, lip prints (LPs), and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The wide range of applications for rare earth (RE) ion-doped phosphors in solid-state lighting and display technologies, such as vacuum fluorescent displays, field emission displays, LEDs, CRTs and display panels, and display panels, has resulted in a substantial increase in research interest in these materials. Luminescence, which is closely related to the structures and compositions of the materials, determines their suitability for use. The remarkable optical characteristics, long fluorescence lifetime, notable Stokes shift, and combination of high luminescence efficiency and strong photochemical stability make RE-ion-doped phosphors stand out [1,2]. Oxide-based host matrices have been thoroughly investigated, particularly as ideal hosts for RE ions like europium (Eu) to produce red light emission [3]. These matrices are well-known for their strong chemical resistance and thermal stability. Strong UV and cathode-ray-excited luminescence is demonstrated by Eu3+ doped yttrium and gadolinium oxides, which qualifies them for use in lamps and displays. To enhance luminescent properties, current research focuses on creating composites by combining these materials, leveraging their similar cubic structures with minimal differences in lattice constants [4–6]. Among various RE ions, Eu3+ emerges as a commonly used activator in phosphors, emitting red light ∼612 nm due to the 5D0→7F2 transition [7,8].

As a new class of carbon-based nanomaterial, CDs have attracted a lot of interest due to their remarkable optical characteristics, low toxicity, affordability, and outstanding biocompatibility [9–12]. Surface modification and heteroatom doping are important tactics to modify the optical characteristics of CDs [13–16]. Fluorescent materials based on CDs have become the best substitute for costly rare-earth-based phosphors in w-LEDs in recent years [17,18]. However, research on CDs based w-LEDs is still in its early stages, facing a challenge with most CDs solid powders exhibiting weak fluorescence due to the self-quenching effect. The use of CDs in w-LEDs is greatly hampered by their lack of solid-state luminescence. Matrix encapsulation is an efficient way to reduce the detrimental aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) consequences. Several studies show that the self-quenching problem may be resolved by embedding solid matrices, such as silicone, starch, borax, silica xerogel, and epoxy resin, which will scatter and protect CDs [19,20]. Thus, finding an appropriate host matrix for CDs is essential to furthering w-LED development.

Composites combining RE luminescent elements and CDs have been studied recently. Liu et al. [21], for example, showed that light modulation in the resultant composites may be achieved by varying the ratio of Eu3+ complex to CDs. This provides a strong basis for the use of CDs in biotechnology, OLED devices, laser technology, and optical amplification. NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites with dual emission properties are shown by Xu and colleagues [22] and represent a good choice to construct a colorimetric fluorescence probe. This technique gives more accuracy than conventional fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the composite shows great promise in multicolor display and w-LED applications by adjusting the ratio of CDs to Eu3+. Their research group next looked into the characteristics and uses of NaYF4:Yb, Er(Tm)@CD composites, offering new perspectives on the creation of up-conversion luminous materials, especially for the biological domain [23].

Moreover, the incorporation of CDs into phosphors has proven effective in enhancing their luminous efficiency [24,25]. For example, the addition of blue CDs coating improved the colorimetric characteristics of Dy3+ doped tellurite and silicate glasses by adding a blue-emitting component [26]. A significant increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity is reported in CDs@ZnGa2O4 composites, which is explained by higher concentrations of disorder and vacancies [27]. The influence of CD integration on the enhancement of phosphor luminescence is investigated using energy transfer methods. Multicolor YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites luminescence is greatly enhanced by energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ [28]. The energy transfer between N-doped CDs (NCDs) and Tm3+ ions is also responsible for the increased luminescence observed with ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites [29]. According to reports, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ is the reason why CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ showed greater PL intensity than YBO3:Eu3+ [30].

The integration of CDs into MAl2O4-based phosphors, such as ZnAl2O4 (ZAO), is pursued to attain specific luminescent features. Hollow ZAO:Eu3+ NPs with high surface areas and superior red emission have been synthesized using CDs as hard templates [31]. ZAO NPs are able to display orange/red and green/yellow phosphorescence thanks to embedded CDs [32,33]. The ZAO matrix, which includes the confinement effect, spin-orbit coupling mechanism, and effective energy transfer from inorganic trap states to CD triplet states, is thought to be responsible for protecting CD triplet emission, which led to the multi-emissive phosphorescence of CDs@ZAO [34]. Although several noteworthy outcomes have been attained, the investigation and utilization of these composite materials are still in their infancy, requiring further study and advancement.

In recent times, there has been a growing interest in the development of optical temperature sensors. Temperature, as a fundamental parameter of thermodynamics, holds significant importance in various fields such as scientific research, industrial manufacturing, and daily activities. To meet the diverse environmental requirements, different types of thermometers with varying measurement ranges have been explored. Among these, thermometry based on the luminescence of rare earth (RE) ions doped materials stands out, as it utilizes the temperature dependence of spectral position, bandwidth, intensity, or lifetime of emission. This technique is particularly promising for situations that require non-contact and non-invasive detection, such as in biological fluids, strong electromagnetic fields, and fast-moving objects [35,36]. Ratio-metric sensing methods, such as fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) thermometry, have garnered significant attention. FIR thermometry relies on the temperature-induced changes in luminescence intensities from two thermally coupled levels (TCL). This method offers high measurement sensitivity and accuracy, as well as superior spatial resolution, as the signal is minimally affected by spectrum losses and fluctuations in pumping intensity.

FPs have a very distinctive physiological structure and characteristic system, making them a typical biometric for personal identification. LFPs are crucial physical evidence in criminal investigations because of their collectability and stable preservation [37,38]. As a result, the visualization of LFPs is becoming a more important area of study. Chemical procedures and physical adsorption are used in traditional fingerprint visualization techniques [39]. Nevertheless, there are disadvantages to chemical techniques, such as high toxicity and slower reaction speeds [40]. Techniques with minimal toxicity, good water resistance, high sensitivity and high stability, are needed for LFP detection [41]. Due to its low toxicity, ease of use, and efficacy, the physical adsorption approach is now the most widely employed for LFP detection; phosphors utilized in LFP detection are included in this method. Although phosphors are widely used in LFP development, the image quality of the studies that are now available is subpar. There is little contrast between fingerprint ridges and grooves, and it is difficult to see details at the I-III level, particularly level 3 features like sweat pores and ridge varieties. Therefore, the present research priority in the field of LFP development include choosing an appropriate phosphor and creating high-quality phosphors with uniform particle size, high luminous intensity, sensitivity and color purity.

Lip prints are recognizable patterns of skin on the points of the lips that are both distinctive and constant, providing a good amount of intricacy for identification. LLPs are the term used to describe the invisible LPs that are left at a crime scene. The lips mucous membrane displays distinct patterns of furrows and indentations, creating lip impressions in the area where the mucosa and skin meet. Similar to FPs, these prints are unique to each individual, permanent, and unchangeable. Environmental influences have little effect on the distribution or shape of wrinkles on lip impressions [42,43]. Powder dusting is a popular technique for detecting LFPs and LPs because of its ease of use, safety, and accessibility [44,45]. Some phosphors have significant benefits over other powders, such as metal and magnetic ones, such as excellent contrast, selectivity, and low background interference [46,47]. As a result, phosphors application in fingerprint and lip print recognition has been a hot topic for study in recent years.

The issue of counterfeiting is expanding and affects a wide range of industries, including important papers, money, branded goods, commercial manufacturing, and even human health. It creates difficult problems with implications for both the economy and public safety. The widespread availability of high-quality devices such as digital cameras, scanners, and printers enables counterfeiters to use personal computers to create flawless copies. These illegal operations endanger not just the welfare of society as a whole but also the rights of lawful businesses, resulting in financial losses. One striking example is medicine counterfeiting, when patients face serious health risks due to unknown or wrong chemical levels. In order to prevent these kinds of incidents and fight counterfeiting, it is essential to create technology that can authenticate papers and brands. The AC techniques that are now in use rely on tags that are cloneable due to their minimal complexity and strong predictability. However, advancements in technology have made it possible to investigate remedies for the problems associated with counterfeiting by creating systems that are simple to construct but difficult to duplicate. Electronic technologies such as RFID tags and sophisticated optical marking techniques including watermarks, holograms, barcodes, box seals, and luminescence printing have become prominent among them. According to recent research, physical unclonable functions (PUFs) can be used to create security labels by utilizing random variations that are included into the process of production. Because the PUF system cannot be copied, only those with the decryption key can access it and read the message, this method guarantees a high degree of security [48–50].

An effort is undertaken to boost the red emission of YGO doped with Eu3+ by varying the content of AVCDs. The investigation included the thermal stability, surface morphology, photoluminescent characteristics, and crystallinity of (YGO):Eu3+ with and without CDs. Furthermore, the optical thermometry features of (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs NCs are explored. The resulting optimized nanocomposites find application in advanced forensic and AC application.

First, the A.V leaves are sliced into pieces and properly cleaned to remove any dust and debris. The A.V gel is then extracted by removing the upper portion of the leaves. The A.V gel is collected with a knife and pulverized in a mortar to make it a thin paste. The collected A.V gel is then mixed with the necessary volume of water and heated at 50 °C for 30 min. This allowed the solution color to change from white to a yellowish-green color. The A.V gel is then allowed to cool to room temperature (RT) in order to prepare it for hydrothermal treatment.

Solution combustion method is used to synthesize YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) nanoparticles. Materials used in the process include Yttrium (III) nitrate hexahydrate [Y(NO3)3.6H2O, 99.99 %], Gadolinium nitrate [Gd(NO3)3.6H2O, 99.99 %], Europium nitrate [Eu(NO3)3, 99.99 %] are procured from Sigma-Aldrich, India and A.V gel used as a bio extract. Three main processes comprise the SCS process: (I) forming the gel, (II) forming the combustion mixture, and (III) burning the gel. A.V gel is added as the fuel (F) after stoichiometric quantities of oxidizers (O), such as Y(NO3)3.6H2O and Eu(NO3)3, are combined in 50 ml of deionized (DI) water. After heating the resultant solution to around 400 °C for 10 min, a foamy YGO:Eu3+ NPs is synthesized within 5 min. To avoid the generation of undesirable compounds, the gel breakdown should preferably take place in a single step, evenly, quickly, and across a limited temperature range. To improve crystallinity and remove carbon residues, the resulting powder is next coarsely crushed and heated in air at 900 °C for 3 h. The several steps in the SC process are shown in Fig. 1 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from aloe vera gel
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of AVCDs, a hydrothermal method is employed. First, 30 ml of A.V gel is heated to 180 °C for 5 h in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After the synthesis period, the autoclave is allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature. Upon opening the Teflon container, a mixture of dark brown powder and a brown solution is observed. To eliminate larger insoluble particles, the brown solution underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min. Filtration is then conducted to further purify the solution. The AVCDs ethanol solution is subsequently enclosed in a dialysis bag (100–500 Da, Solar bio). This sealed dialysis bag, immersed in ethanol, underwent gentle magnetic stirring. Over time, alkali migrated gradually from the interior to the exterior of the dialysis bag due to osmotic pressure. Ethanol replacement occurred every 6 h until no alkali is detected in the ethanol, monitored using an ion conductivity meter. Ultimately, the AVCDs are retained within the dialysis bag. After lyophilization, powdered AVCDs are obtained for further studies. Fig. 1b illustrates a schematic representation of the AVCDs derived from A.V gel.

To create the composite, YGO:Eu3+ NPs is synthesized using the combustion method. A Petri dish is used to take a fine powder of YGO:Eu3+. AVCDs, prepared via the hydrothermal method, are added to this powder, and the mixture underwent homogenization through ultrasonication for 30 min. The homogenized mixture is then put in an oven for 12 h at 90 °C. After then, RT is reached by the resultant composite (Fig. 1c). The composite is created and used for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of latent fingerprints and lip prints using optimized nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: A 25-year-old male/female donor provided LFPs and LPs with a loop-shaped pattern. Prior to applying pressure to different surfaces, the fingertips are made sure to be clean. During the method, the right thumb finger is used to softly contact the forehead. It is then pressed against various substrates, such as metal, glass, CD, and aluminum foil, for a short period of time before being lifted from the substrate surface. Optimized nanocomposite is applied to the LFPs with a gentle brush (Fig. 1d). The resultant latent fingerprint images are taken with a Canon EOS 7D Mark III DSLR digital camera in both daylight and UV 365 nm light.

In this process, 30 mL of deionized (DI) water is utilized to dissolve 2 gm of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with an average molecular weight of 125,000 g/mol. The solution is obtained by cooling it to room temperature (RT) and stirring it with a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until it became clear. Subsequently, to ensure thorough mixing, the appropriate volume of the PVA solution is added to the prepared YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs and subjected to probe sonication for 30 min. The resulting solution, comprising the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs (2 mg) and PVA (0.5 mg) mixture, served as AC ink for information encryption. A schematic representation of the development of AC labels is depicted in Fig. 1e.

The synthesized YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs is combined with 0.1 gm of UV glue in order to construct the red LED, which is set up with a 395 nm UV chip. The green phosphor (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+, the blue phosphor BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+, and the red phosphor YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs are combined in a 1:1:10 ratio to create a combination for the w-LED. The combined weight of the three phosphors is 0.05 g. The glue/phosphor mixes are well mixed in an agate mortar before being put to a 395 nm UV chip and baked for 5 min under 395 nm UV light irradiation.

Several tools are used in this investigation to characterize the data. (i) Powder XRD patterns of the prepared materials are obtained using a Shimadzu diffractometer (CuKα = 1.5406 Å, 45 kV, 200 mA). The scanning rate is 2°/min, and the 2θ range was adjusted between 10 and 80°. Using FullProf software, Rietveld refinement was used to examine how the concentration of Eu3+ doping affected the cubic structure based on the XRD profiles that are produced. (ii) An Esca lab 250Xi spectrometer was used to conduct X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). (iii) A PerkinElmer (λ35) spectrophotometer was used to record the diffuse reflectance (DR) spectra of the prepared NPs and NCs. (iv) The microstructure and elemental composition are examined using an EDAX Apollo XT EDS system in conjunction with a JEOL-JEM-2100 PLUS SEM. (v) TEM, HRTEM images, and microscopic chemical composition are produced with the JEM-2010 TEM system. (vi) A micro-Raman spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm was utilized to perform Raman spectroscopy. (vii) Excitation and emission spectra are recorded at room temperature using a Horiba spectrofluorometer equipped with a 450 W Xenon lamp for photoluminescence investigation. PluoroLog 3-TCSPC was used to gather PL lifetime data and to estimate the absolute quantum yield (QY) using an integrating sphere.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the XRD patterns of un-doped, YGO: Eu3+ (1–9 mol %), AVCDs, and YGO: Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs. In the YGO: Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) NPs, all the diffraction peaks match with the JCPDS card numbers 83–0927 (Y2O3) and 76–0155 (Gd2O3) [51]. No impurity peaks are observed, indicating successful doping of Eu3+ into the YGO host lattice by replacing Gd3+. The XRD pattern of the AVCDs sample exhibits a broad peak centered at 2θ = 21.8°, corresponding to the carbon-based material structure [52,53]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of YGO:7Eu3+ and AVCDs are simultaneously observed in the YGO: Eu3+ sample. Furthermore, the average crystallite size (D) of YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) NPs can be calculated using Scherrer's relation [54];

In the above relation, β represents the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in radians of the strongest peak in XRD profiles, λ = 1.540 Å corresponds to the X-ray wavelength (CuKα: 1.540 Å), k: shape factor (0.941), and θ denotes the diffraction angle. The calculated crystallite sizes are observed to range from 34 to 54 nm. Additionally, the Williamson-Hall (W–H) method [55] was employed to estimate the crystallite size (D), dislocation density (δ), stacking fault (SF), and micro-strain (ε) in the lattice using the following relation [56];

The intercept of the linearly fitted line gives the crystallite size. The obtained graphs are shown in Fig. 2b. The estimated values of D, δ, SF and ε are given in Table S1.

Fig. 2c displays the refinement data, featuring the green line (calculated data), blue cross (raw data), and brown line (difference), processed using the FullProf program. For the optimized YGO:7Eu3+ NPs, the fitted results show χ2 = 1.76 (reduced chi-square), Rp = 52.5 % (residual of least squares), and Rwp = 41.7 % (weighted residual or goodness of fit). These values suggest a well-refined crystal structure, confirming the reliable substitution of Eu3+ ions for Gd3+ ions in YGO NPs, as summarized in Table 1 . Refined atomic parameters, positions and inter-atomic bond distances for un-doped and YGO:7Eu3+ NPs are listed in Tables 2 and 3
. In order to develop the 3D crystal structure of YGO:Eu3+ NPs, additional data acquired through the refinement procedure in the form of a cif file was processed using Diamond 4.5.2 software. This included the coordination environment of cationic sites within the unit cell, as depicted in Fig. 2d, along with structural parameters (α = β = γ = 90° and a = b = c = 10.713 Å). The unit cell parameters for un-doped and YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) NPs are compared in Table 1. It was found that the addition of different concentrations of Eu3+ ions had a little impact on certain metrics, such the doped materials volume, which dropped from 3961.56 to 3949.56 Å3. The following formula was used to calculate the ionic radius difference (Δr) of the cations by activator ions [57]:

The effective ionic radii of the host and activator cations, along with their coordination number (CN), are represented in the equation by Rh (CN) and Rd (CN). By simplifying the equation, we may obtain the deduced Δr as follows: 51.11 % for Y3+ (ionic radius, r = 104 p.m.) and Eu3+ (r = 108.7 p.m.); likewise, 15.70 % (admissible value < 30 %) for Gd3+ (r = 107.8 p.m.) and Eu3+. This clearly shows that in the solid solution, Gd3+ ions are dislocated in Eu3+ ions. Tables 1 and 2 additionally provide specific refined characteristics such bond lengths and lattice constants.

The electron density (ED) of the most intense plane is shown in Fig. 2e and f. ED mapping was performed using the GFourier software from the FullProf package [58], which yielded information on the distribution of ED inside the unit cell. Determining the precise position of atoms within the unit cell requires an analysis of the electron density. The Fourier transform of the geometrical structural component applied to the whole unit cell is known as the ED [58]. A two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) Fourier map is used to depict the electron scattering density. It is defined as a periodic function of position, peaking at the precise position of the atom and dropping to a minimum value in the space between atoms. In this case, a zone with a high ED is shown by blue, while a zone with a low ED is indicated by yellow. In order to better understand chemical bonding and charge transfer in the YGO:7Eu3+ complex, 2D-Fourier maps are frequently created as contours that show the ED distribution surrounding each atom of the different constituents of the compound. Thick and dense ED curves suggest that the unit cell contains a significantly heavier element.

A single ED level is shown by the 3D-Fourier maps, which combine to produce a matrix resembling chicken wire. The unit cell xy plane (z = 0) displays the 2D ED distribution of the Y/Gd/Eu and O atoms, as seen in Fig. 2e. The distribution of electrons in valence d orbitals may be responsible for the thick and dense circular contours around Y/Gd, whereas the distribution of electrons in valence 2s and 2p orbitals may be responsible for the contours surrounding O. In Fig. 3 e, the ED levels are indicated by the colored region around Y/Gd and O, while the zero-level density contour is represented by the yellow color. The 3D Fourier ED maps of the elements Y, Gd, Eu, and O in the unit cell of YGO:7Eu3+ at z = 0 are shown in Fig. 2f. The quantity of electrons enveloping an atom affects how X-rays scatter. Since Y/Gd atoms have more electrons than Eu atoms, it is predicted that they will scatter more X-rays, giving Y more strong peaks than Eu. Using the FullProf program, comparable findings on ED mapping for oxides have been documented and examined in recent literature [59].

Section 4.2 delves into morphology analysis, examining the SEM images depicted in Figure S2a-j for the un-doped, YGO:Eu3+ (1–9) NPs, AVCDs, and AVCDs@YGO:7Eu3+ (1–5 wt%) NCs. The un-doped and YGO:Eu3+ (1–9) NPs showcase agglomerated spherical particles, while AVCDs exhibit quasi-spherical shape, with particle agglomeration increasing upon encapsulating CDs into YGO:7Eu3+ NPs (Figure S2g-j). The particle size distribution, ranging from 20 to 40 nm, is illustrated in Figure S1a1-j1. EDS analysis in Fig. 3a confirms the elemental composition of YGO:7Eu3+, displaying Y, Gd, Eu, and O with atomic percentages (inset Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b shows the FESEM image of YGO:7Eu3+ NPs. Elemental mapping in Fig. 3c–f validates the uniform distribution of elements (Y, Gd, Eu, and O), represented by red, green, white, and cyan blue colors. TEM images in Fig. 3g–i reveal the optimal sample, confirming its nano size within the range of 20–25 nm. TEM and HRTEM analyses in Fig. 3h provide insights into the morphology and size distribution of AVCDs. These particles exhibit a quasi-spherical shape with a narrow, uniform size distribution, averaging between 6 and 10 nm. The inset in Fig. 3h reveals distinct lattice fringes, with a calculated spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100) facet. Moving to Fig. 3i, the TEM image of YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs displays well-dispersed quasi-spherical AVCDs, with an average particle size of ∼5 nm (inset image). HR-TEM image of the optimized nanocomposite showcases clear fringe widths matching d values from XRD data (Fig. 3j). Notably, diffraction planes (002) and (222) with inter-planar spacing of 0.37 nm and 0.30 nm, respectively, are marked in the image.

Figure S3a illustrates the diffuse reflection spectra of un-doped, YGO:Eu3+ (1–9) NPs, and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3, 5 wt %) NCs, showcasing high reflectance of approximately 70 % in the 350–800 nm range. Weak bands at 399 nm (7F0→5L6), 558 nm (7F0→5D1), and 595 nm (7F0→5D0) are observed, attributed to meta-stable energy levels resulting from f-f transitions of Eu3+ ions in YGO [60]. By analyzing the diffuse reflectance spectra transformation, the energy band-gap (Eg) of un-doped, YGO:Eu3+ (1–9) NPs, and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3, 5 wt %) NCs is estimated. The Eg values can be evaluated using the provided equation [61].

The co-efficient of reflection (R) is used in the equation to determine the energy band gap (Eg). For un-doped, YGO:7Eu3+ NPs, and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3, 5 wt %) NCs, Eg values are found to be 5.35, 5.46, and 5.34–5.75 eV, respectively (Figure S3b). The spectral bands in the profile indicate a noticeable blue shift in absorption bands, corresponding to increased band gap values with the addition of AVCDs. The elevated band gap for YGO:7Eu3+ NCs after AVCDs addition can be explained by the Burstein-Moss effect [63], observed in heavily doped oxides. This effect, arising from impurity carriers filling the conduction band, pushes the Fermi level into the conduction band, requiring higher energy for photon-induced electron transitions between the valence and conduction bands.

In Figure S4a, the XPS survey scan confirms the presence of Y, Gd, Eu, and O elements in YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs. High-resolution XPS spectra in Figure S4b-f depicts Y 3d, Gd 4d, O 1s, Eu 3d, and C 1s. Y 3d spectrum (Figure S4b) exhibits peaks at 156.13 and 158.14 eV, corresponding to Y 3d5/2 and Y 3d3/2, respectively [64]. Gd 4d spectrum (Figure S4c) shows peaks at 141.5 and 147.7 eV, confirming the Gd3+ valence state. O 1s (Figure S4d) corresponds to an oxidation state of −2 with a binding energy of 531.13 eV. Eu 3d (Figure S4e) exhibits an oxidation state of +3 with doublet spin-orbit coupling states of 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 at 1124.60 and 1154.47 eV. Normalization using the C 1s binding energy peak at 284 eV is depicted in Figure S4f.

Fig. 4 a showcasing the PLE spectra of YGO:7Eu3+ examined at a wavelength of 612 nm. The spectra exhibit a band ∼270 nm is attributed to the O2−-Eu3+ charge transfer band (CTB). Notably, strong absorption peaks within 360–480 nm arise from the 4f-4f transition of Eu3+, conforming to transitions 7F0→5L8 (363 nm), 7F0→5G2 (382 nm), 7F0→5L6 (395 nm), 7F0→5D3 (416 nm), and 7F0→5D2 (465 nm) [65]. The 395 nm excitation wavelength aligns with UV chips, suggesting the applicability of YGO:7Eu3+ in w-LEDs. Fig. 4b illustrates the emission spectra of YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) phosphor upon 395 nm excitation. Typical peaks appear in the 500–720 nm range, which are ascribed to the electronic transitions of Eu3+ ions at 5D0→7Fn (n = 1, 2, 3, and 4). The magnetic-dipole (MD) transition occurring at 593 nm (5D0→7F1) is not influenced by the host environment. Induced electric-dipole (ED) transitions give rise to transitions at 612 nm (5D0→7F2) and 707 nm (5D0→7F4). Additionally, the 655 nm transition (5D0→7F3) is a forbidden transition, disregarded due to its non-compliance with the selection rules of ED or MD transition. The location and form of the emission peaks seldom alter as the doping concentration rises. The emission intensity increases at first, then decreases, peaking at a doping concentration of 7 mol% Eu3+ ions, which is explained by concentration quenching (CQ). This phenomenon can be elucidated through multipolar interactions, where the critical distance (Rc) plays a pivotal role. CQ occurs when Rc > 5 Å due to multipolar connections; otherwise, it arises from exchange interactions. Equation (6), based on Blasse's theory [66], is employed to calculate Rc value.
(6)

The equation, where V represents the unit cell volume, N is the number of cations, and xc is the critical concentration, is applied with values V = 1180.53 Å³, N = 4, and xc = 0.07 in the YGO:7Eu3+ matrix. Consequently, Rc is calculated as 10.2 Å, significantly exceeding the 5 Å threshold. This suggests that the energy transfer of Eu3+ is predominantly influenced by the multipole-multipole interaction process. Additionally, the Dexter's theory [67] is employed to CQ through equation (7);

Fig. 4d illustrates the correlation between log(I/x) and log(x) for YGO:7Eu3+. A fitted curve yields a slope of −1.89, predicting a Q value of 5.67, close to the expected value of 6. This suggests that the primary cause for the concentration quenching in YGO:7Eu3+ is the dipole-dipole interaction. Fig. 4e presents the energy level scheme of YGO:7Eu3+, effectively illustrating the PLE and PL spectra. The YGO:7Eu3+ phosphor demonstrates many PLE peaks in the UV region that are associated with f-f transitions. Electrons absorb energy from the ground state 5H7 level upon excitation at 395 nm, migrate to the excited state level, and then undergo non-radiative transitions (NR) to transition to the 5D0 level. Subsequently, electrons relax to the ground state through radiative transitions, producing emission peaks at 593, 612, 655, and 708 nm, attributed to 5D0→7FJ (J = 1, 2, 3 & 4) respectively.

The AVCDs ratio was varied from 1, 3, 5 and 7 wt % and highest intensity is achieved for 5 wt % and for 7 wt % the intensity declines (Fig. 5 a). PLE and PL spectra of YGO:Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) was analyzed to examine the energy absorption and emission properties (Fig. 5b). The PLE peak of Eu3+ at 395 nm was well correlated with the wide PL spectrum produced by AVCDs (Fig. 5c). The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO, π*) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO, π) energy levels in CDs are thought to be the source of these PLE peaks in AVCDs. Red emission was produced by the luminescence of AVCDs, which was mainly caused by the π* transition in the core states. But surface state existence restricted the energy levels, leading to matching π* electron transitions producing broadband red emission. The combination of core and surface state emissions yielded an asymmetric PL spectral pattern, with PL peaks observed at 490 nm for AVCDs. Effective energy transfer is made possible by the large spectrum overlap that can be achieved between the PLE of phosphors and the PL of AVCDs owing to this broad PL spectra (Fig. 5c) [68,69].

In Fig. 5d, the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of YGO:7Eu3+, YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3, 5 and 7 wt%) under 395 nm excitation reveal distinct emission peaks corresponding to Eu3+ ions in the recorded samples. Notably, YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs exhibits a superior spectral overlap compared to YGO:7Eu3+. This enhanced spectral overlap enables more efficient excitation energy transfer from AVCDs to YGO:7Eu3+. AVCDs efficiently absorb the 395 nm excitation, which is then converted to Eu3+ by Förster or Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism and released via radiative emission. Consequently, YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) has PL intensity 4 times greater than YGO:7Eu3+ (Fig. 5d). The energy transmission mechanisms between AVCDs and the Eu3+ activator is depicted in Fig. 5e. Red emission is caused by electron transitions of Eu3+ from the 7F0→5L6 energy level, followed by non-radiative relaxation to 5D0, and ultimately to 7F0–4. This process occurs under 395 nm photon stimulation. In addition to absorbing photon energy, excited electrons in AVCDs also undergo non-radiative transitions from HOMO to LUMO, relax to core and surface state energy levels, and finally move via the FRET process to the 5D0–2 energy levels of Eu3+.

Evaluating the stability of YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs composites is crucial for their practical application in w-LEDs. The effects of air, heat, and UV exposure on the optical properties of the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs composite and the original AVCDs are comprehensively examined. To prevent fluorescence quenching from aggregation, AVCDs stability is tested in an aqueous solution. A portable UV lamp emitting 2 W/cm2 of UV light is used to continuously expose samples to test their resistance to UV radiation. Fig. 6 a & b shows the PL spectra of UV irradiated AVCDs (5 wt %) and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs. Fig. 6c illustrates the variation in PL intensity for AVCDs and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs NCs over different irradiation durations. AVCDs exhibit nearly a 50 % decrease in fluorescence intensity due to the photo bleaching effect. In contrast, the composites demonstrate superior stability, retaining 90 % of the original PL intensity after 240 min of irradiation. The 10 % reduction is attributed to photo oxidation, involving the generation of superoxide species, surface oxidation, water production, yttrium-based oxides slow hydration, and eventual material lattice destruction. In order to confirm the thermal stability of YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs composites and AVCDs solutions against the heat produced during w-LED operation, they are heated to 100 °C and allowed to cool naturally for 7 cycles. Fig. 6 d & e shows the PL spectra of UV irradiated AVCDs (5 wt %) and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs. Fig. 6f reveals a 7 % reduction in PL intensity for AVCDs, while the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs exhibit nearly unchanged PL intensity after 7 heating-cooling cycles, indicating superior thermal stability. In an air atmosphere (Temperature: 18–22 °C and humidity: 50–80 %) for 15 days, AVCDs show evident PL intensity degradation, whereas, YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs NCs exhibit minimal changes (Fig. 6g and h). This robust stability underscores the potential application of YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs NCs in w-LEDs.

The PL data of the obtained samples are excited under 395 nm and then transformed into CIE coordinates (Fig. 7 a). Notably, the CIE coordinates for YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) NPs are positioned at the edge of the red region, and their values are detailed in Table 4 . However, AVCDs exhibited a distinctly pure yellow emission with CIE coordinates (0.3265, 0.6702). For YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3 & 5 wt %), the CIE coordinates are determined as (0.6458, 0.351), (0.6514, 0.349), and (0.6555, 0.339), respectively. These findings reveal the tunability of YGO matrix luminescence by incorporating Eu3+ and AVCDs content. Ultimately, the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs NCs achieving white light emission is attained. The CIE chromaticity coordinates for YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol %) and YGO:7Eu3+ @AVCDs (1, 3, & 5 wt%) NCs are depicted in Fig. 7a. The figure illustrates a shift in CIE coordinates from orange-red to deep red with an increase in AVCDs content. Furthermore, to assess the color purity, the YGO:Eu3+ (1–9 mol%) NPs and YGO:7Eu3+ @AVCDs (1, 3, & 5 wt%) NCs can be quantified using formula (9) [70].

Through the fitting calculation, the average lifetimes of YGO: 7Eu3+, and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs are 1.80 and 2.01 ns, respectively, which is lower than that of pure AVCDs, indicating the existence of energy transfer between AVCDs and YGO:7Eu3+ NPs.

where η is the internal quantum efficiency, ES and ER are the excitation spectral lines of the phosphor and BaSO4, and LS is the spectral emission line of YGO:7Eu3+ and YGO:7Eu3+ @AVCDs. Fig. 7d illustrates that the IQE at 395 nm excitation is 63.21 % for YGO:7Eu3+ and 74.16 % for YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs. In comparison with YGO:7Eu3+ NPs, YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs exhibit superior quantum efficiency and color purity. These findings underscore the suitability of the optimized composite as a material for w-LEDs.

Given that LEDs generate heat during operation, phosphors with high thermal stability are preferable in this field [74]. The emission spectra of YGO:7Eu3+ NPs and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs at various temperatures are calculated (Fig. 8 a and b). Thermal stability analysis, conducted at 395 nm excitation, reveals a decrease in luminescence intensities within the range of 300–480 K (Fig. 8c). Even at 480 K, there is a slight variation in luminescence intensity, although it is still greater than at ambient temperature (300 K). This little alteration suggests that there is virtually no thermal quenching occurrence in the NPs and NCs. However, when exposed to higher temperatures than ambient temperature (300 K), many phosphors lose some of their luminosity. Examples of these include Y2Mo4O15:Sm3+ (66.7 %), La2MoO6:Sm3+ (47.5 %), and NaSrLa(MoO4)3:Sm3+ (48 %), all of which observed at 420 K [75–77]. According to Fig. 8c as the temperature reaches at 423 K, the residual luminescence intensity of YGO: 7Eu3+ NPs (85.15 %) and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs (88.31 %), which is much greater than that of Y2O3:Eu3+ (81.98 %) and Sr2Si5N8:Eu2+ NPs (81.98 %). Moreover, it is much greater than many reported phosphors. Table 5 presents the comparison between different phosphors [78–85]. The result illustrates that the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs has outstanding thermal stability.

Fig. 8d and e depict the relationship between ln [(I0/I)-1] and 1/kBT. Through fitting, the activation energy (Ea) can be calculated using the Arrhenius formula (11) [86]:

In the Arrhenius formula (11), where I0 represents the initial luminescence intensity of the phosphor, I(T) is the emission intensity of YGO:7Eu3+ NPs and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs at different temperatures, c denotes the Boltzmann constant, and Ea indicates the activation energy. The calculated Ea values are 0.19 eV for YGO: 7Eu3+ NPs and 0.20eV for YGO: 7Eu3+ @AVCDs (5 wt%), demonstrating superior thermal stability compared to La2Zr2O7:Eu3+ (298−573 K: Ea = 0.16) BaTiO3:Eu3+ (300−475 K: Ea = 0.088) [87,88]. These results further emphasize the exceptional thermal stability of the optimized nanocomposite. The chromaticity co-ordinates of the YGO:7Eu3+ NPs and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs in Fig. 8f hardly shift from 300 to 480 K. The small standard deviations (YGO:7Eu3+ NPs: Δx = 1.166 × 10−8, Δy = 1.111 × 10−8) and (YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs: Δx = 8.177 × 10−8, Δy = 8.844 × 10−8) suggest that the prepared NCs have high color thermal stability.

The variation of I593/I612 intensity ratios with temperature of is presented in the inset of Fig. 8g and h, respectively. The ln(FIR) variation with 1/T for the two FIR values is depicted in Fig. 8g and h. The slope of the ln(FIR) vs 1/T plot gives the values of (ΔE/k), which are 414.133 eV (YGO:7Eu3+) and 480.01 eV (YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs for I593/I612, respectively.

where Sa and Sa represent the absolute and relative sensitivities, respectively, and the dependence of Sa and Sr on temperature for I593/I612 YGO:7Eu3+ and (YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs is depicted in Fig. 8i and j, respectively. The Sr (max) at 300 K for YGO:7Eu3+ and (YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs is 3.94 and 4.67 % K−1, respectively. There is a significant contrast in the two Sr values; the maximum is obtained for the MCDs(5 wt%)@ZAO:7Eu3+ NCs. This is because the 612 nm peak shows a larger variation in temperature than the 593 nm peak (Fig. 8i and j). The high Sr value indicates the good signal-sensing capability of the nanocomposite.

Fig. 8k illustrates the electroluminescence spectrum of the w-LED, which was fabricated by applying a specific blend of green phosphor (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+, blue phosphor BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+ (BAM), and red YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs onto a 398 nm chip. The resulting w-LEDs exhibit a desirable correlated color temperature (CCT) of 5012 K. The inset of Fig. 8k presents the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the fabricated w-LED alongside the CIE coordinates of a standard white light source. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of the w-LED are positioned at (0.343, 0.331), demonstrating close proximity to the standard white light spot coordinates (0.333, 0.333). The inset displays a photograph of the w-LED. At a driving voltage of 4 V and a current of 350 mA, the warm white emission is clearly visible to the naked eye. The fabricated w-LED exhibits a bright white light with a remarkable luminous efficacy reaching up to 20.21 lm W−1, indicating the successful packaging of the w-LED device.

Fig. 9 presents a straightforward sensitivity comparison of YGO:7Eu3+ and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3 & 5 wt %) NCs. The developed FPs on glass surface are visualized under day light and UV 365 nm (Fig. 9a and b). While observing FPs under 365 nm UV exposure, the YGO:7Eu3+ and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3 wt %) NCs offer a lower-quality and less contrasted image than the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs covered LFP. Since the FPs minute features met the requirements for FP recognition, extremely luminescent YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs may be a viable option for LFP detection. The pixel profile of the chosen area of the matching FPs, denoted by the white line in Fig. 9c, is displayed for more realistic purposes. The line intensity profile curve makes it easy to distinguish between the FPs ridges and furrows. A pixel profile value that differs noticeably from the FP guarantees that YGO:7Eu3+ and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (1, 3, 5 wt %) NCs can distinguish LFPs from various objects. The uniform distribution of NPs and NCs along the fingerprint ridges is confirmed by the 3D interactive plots [90,91] of the matching FPs, as shown in Fig. 9c.

To assess the applicability of phosphors in detecting LFPs on various surfaces, YGO:7Eu3+ @AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs are employed to visualize FPs on different objects, including Al. foil, CD surfaces, metal materials, and glass surfaces. Fig. 10 a displays the FPs images on these diverse substrates visualized under UV light. Regardless of the substrate material, the FPs are distinctly revealed when coated with YGO: 7Eu3+ @AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs. This FP visualization experiment across different materials serves as a practical reference for the potential application of the optimized NCs. Consequently, YGO: 7Eu3+ @AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs emerges as a promising material for LFP detection. To further evaluate the effectiveness of the developed FPs, pixel analyses of the rectangle region are used (Fig. 10b). The excellent resolution of LFP detection is demonstrated by the red values, with minimum background interference clearly differentiate the grooves and ridges. Hence, YGO: 7Eu3+ @AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs exhibits significant potential for LFP detection. Fig. 10c illustrates 3D interactive plots of the corresponding FPs, confirming the uniform distribution of the optimized NCs over the ridges of the FPs [92,93].

To validate the efficacy of the NCs in LFP detection, YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs are employed for FP development on an aluminum glass surface. FP features, based on microscopic details, are categorized into 3 levels: level-I traits (basic shapes), level-II traits (local minutiae points), and level-III traits (scar and sweat-pores). For FP recognition, level-II and level-III characteristics are used, but the former is more common and developed. Although the level-III traits hold more individualized information, their extraction necessitates high-resolution fingerprint images. NPs frequently attach themselves to organic residues in LFPs, such as fatty acids, amino acids, and peptides. Photographs of LFPs developed by YGO:7Eu3+ and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs are shown in Fig. 11 a and b. The FPs ridges and grooves are well defined by the results, making it possible to identify level-I traits like core and delta. Level-II features, including bifurcation, island, ridge end, short ridge, eye, dot, enclosure, hook, crossover, and double bifurcation, are also evident. Level-III features, including sweat pores, are discernible [94]. These results establish the ability of YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs to clearly show FP structures at the level I-III. The experiment demonstrates how LFPs on glass may be seen using red phosphor, which allows for a clear distinction between the FPs ridges and furrows. YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs substantially increases the visualization of LFPs, displaying high sensitivity in their detection. The distinct structural patterns of the FP, illustrated through 3D interactive plots generated using YGO: 7Eu3+ and YGO: 7Eu3+ @AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs, are clearly presented in Fig. 11c and d. As can be seen from the images, all three levels of the fingerprint are distinctly observable, emphasizing that YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs serve as a viable option for LFP detection visualization.

In the comprehensive analysis for personal identification, various traits are considered, including number, position, inter-spacing, sweat-pore area, shape of the pore. The average number of pores per unit length serves as a crucial parameter for pore calculation. Fig. 12 a illustrates the number of pores assessed in image developed using YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs via powder dusting approach. In the present case, the number of pores per unit of friction ridge length is considered to be 8. Sweat pores manifest variations in their positions, being either centrally located or opening at one end or both ends. It is crucial to note that the adherence of NCs at the pore edge influences whether a sweat pore appears open or closed. Factors such as fingerprint deposition pressure, nanoparticle homogeneity, and print development procedures can lead to variations in pore types. Consequently, two pores extracted from the same finger of the same person are unlikely to be identical. Fig. 12b illustrates pores on the ridges of the developed FPs on the glass surface. The pictures demonstrate that closed pores are more common than open pores, whether they are present on one or both sides. The shape, size, and type of a pore can alter as it deforms, as in the case of stretching brought on by the elasticity and compression of the skin [95]. The distances between pores, however, continue to be rather constant. Furthermore, the relative positions of pores stay the same during flexion even in the presence of distortions caused by pressure or deviations in the ridge course. As a result, everyone can consistently measure the inter-distance between pores, which is the separation between the centers of two pores. Sweat pores on the same ridge unite to generate a distinct pattern that is particular to each individual. In a similar vein, joining pore centers on adjacent ridges results in a unique design. The pore inter-spacing on marble, aluminum foil, and a leaf under various experimental settings is shown in Fig. 12c. Pores exhibit various shapes, including circles, triangles, and squares, among others, with diverse sizes. However, pore shapes are not constant, as pressure distortions can alter their fundamental forms. The developed FP pore shapes on glass are illustrated in Fig. 12d, providing a list of different pore shapes observed. Despite prior claims in studies [96] suggesting that circular pores are the most common, current data indicate the prevalence of circular, triangular, and square pores, while rhomboid and rectangular pores are less frequent. The examination reveals the presence of round, oval, square, and triangular pores. The angle between consecutive sweat pores and the area of the sweat pores are depicted in Fig. 12e. The fingerprints developed using YGO:7Eu3+ and YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs are aged up to 270 days under ambient condition are shown in Figure S5a & b. After storing for different time, the fluorescent LFPs do not change significantly compared with day 1 to day 270. For increasing fingerprint age, the substructure of LFPs can still be detected even at an age of 270 days, the LFP can still be visualized well by the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs compared to YGO:7Eu3+ NPs, which demonstrates that a low detection limit can be achieved by the NCs. Figure S5c & d shows the pixel profiles of the selected regions on FPs (yellow strip). The furrow and ridge areas of the fingerprints can be clearly distinguished. It illustrates the YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs is a good material to be applied to the visualization of LFP detection and will promote personal identification.

Beyond monozygotic twins, the uniqueness of each persons lip print, or cheiloscopy, is a significant aspect. Unlike palatal rugae and FPs that persist throughout life, LPs offer distinct patterns. Considering this, a comprehensive study is conducted on lip patterns from both male and female donors. Initially nearly invisible under normal light, LPs revealed well-defined groove patterns without background interference upon UV light excitation (Fig. 14a). The nano-sized nature of the NCs and their superior adhesive properties to lip secretions contribute to this enhanced visibility. The distinctive patterns of LPs, influenced by the relaxation of lip muscles, remained visible with persistent visualization sensitivity on both portions, showcasing clear groove patterns. Moreover, the ability of the optimized NCs to reveal detailed groove patterns based on Suzuki and Tsuchihashi [97] classification on a glass surface is investigated (Fig. 14b). The results display the observation of all type I–V groove details, underscoring the versatile nature of the NCs. This capability proves effective in revealing intricate groove patterns, benefiting the personal identification process. The obtained results demonstrate the notable efficiency of the prepared NCs in imaging complete LPs for cheiloscopy applications. From the observations, the male LPs exhibit the following pattern order: Type VI > Type V > Type III > Type II > Type IV > Type I (least common), while female LPs follow the order: Type I followed by Type IV > Type VI > Type V > Type III > Type I > Type II (least common). The distribution of lip print types is illustrated quadrant-wise. Remarkably, Type V prints are almost equally common on both sexes upper lips, despite notable variations in the lower lips of males and females. Focusing solely on the lower lip highlights this discrepancy, suggesting a significantly greater sex difference in Type IV frequency between males and females. These findings underscore the uniqueness of each lip print pattern, offering novel perspectives in the application of nanocomposites for visualizing lip print patterns in forensic sciences [98].

The YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt %) NCs synthesized in this study open up new possibilities for practical data encryption applications due to their exceptional PL properties. Addressing challenges related to counterfeiting and fabrication of essential goods and documents, such as certificates, currency, popular brands, pharmaceuticals, and more, is crucial for global security and socio-economic development. Despite the use of various fluorescent materials in AC applications, persistent issues like toxicity, spectral overlap, low quantum efficiency, and luminescence quenching remain [99–102]. To illustrate the applicability of the synthesized nanocomposites for AC purposes, luminescent-based security inks are fabricated. AC designs are produced using a simple brush mode. The schematic representation of AC label development is shown in Fig. 13 a. The AC labels, fabricated on diverse surfaces (wood, OHP, glossy sheet, steel), are visualized under both daylight and UV 365 nm light (Fig. 13b–d). These patterns exhibit distinct, sharp luminescence that is invisible to the naked eye in ambient light. These findings affirm the high utility of the fabricated ink for UV and thermally sensitive smart labels in data encryption applications.

In conclusion, YGO:Eu3+ nanoparticles (1–9 mol %) are successfully synthesized through the SC method. The highest luminescence intensity is achieved in YGO:7Eu3+ NPs. The effective dispersion and protection of AVCDs within the YGO host enable them to overcome the adverse AIQ effect in the solid state, making them the primary luminescent centers in YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs NCs. Furthermore, the observed energy transfer between Eu3+ and AVCDs significantly contributes to the PL enhancement of YGO:7Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) NCs. Experimental results demonstrate that optimized NCs exhibit outstanding thermal stability and white light emission upon 365 nm excitation. A powder dusting method, employed for visualizing LFPs and LLPs under 365 nm excitation, revealed Level I-III ridge features and Type I-VI groove patterns on various surfaces. The optimized NCs is utilized for developing AC labels on diverse substrates. The developed AC images exhibit superior fluorescence properties and good binding specificity, providing increased contrast and high sensitivity. The overall results clearly demonstrated that the optimized NCs exhibit great potential for applications in solid-state devices, latent fingerprints, lip print and anti-counterfeiting applications.
